14.1 The Mitochondrion: Scene of the Action

Overview of Oxidative Energy Generation (1 of 3)
Energy generation via metabolic oxidation of organic substrates occurs in three stages:
In stage 1, carbon from metabolic fuels is incorporated into acetyl-C o A
In stage 2, the citric acid cycle oxidizes acetyl-CoA to produce
reduced electron carriers, and a small amount of A T P
In stage 3, the reduced electron carriers are reoxidized, providing energy for the synthesis of additional A T P

Overview of Oxidative Energy Generation (2 of 3)
Relatively little A T P is generated per mole of glucose in stages 1 and 2 (including glycolysis and citric acid cycle)
Stages 1 and 2, however, produce 10 moles N A D H and 2
moles of F A D to
Reoxidation of N A D H and
in stage 3 (cellular
respiration, oxidative phosphorylation) provides most of the energy used for A T P synthesis
Stages 2 and 3 as well as part of stage 1 occur in mitochondria

Overview of Oxidative Energy Generation (3 of 3)

14.2 Free Energy Changes in Biological Oxidations

Reduction Potential and Free Energy Changes (1 of 3)
The standard free energy change for a redox reaction is directly related to the difference in reduction potentials
The redox reaction will be energetically favorable (will yield a negative ΔG°′) when the change in the standard reduction potentials is positive (when E°′acceptor > E°′donor)

Reduction Potential and Free Energy Changes (2 of 3)
Consider the overall transfer of electrons from N  A  D  H to oxygen in the respiratory chain given by the equation:

Reduction Potential and Free Energy Changes (3 of 3)
The standard free energy change is highly exergonic (sufficient to drive the synthesis of about 2.5 A T P molecules):

14.3 Electron Transport

Electron Carriers in the Respiratory Chain (1 of 3)
Figure 14.3 Respiratory electron carriers in the mitochondrion.
The respiratory chain catalyzes the flow of electrons from low reduction potential carriers to high reduction potential carriers (exergonic)

Electron Carriers in the Respiratory Chain (2 of 3)
Figure 14.3 Respiratory electron carriers in the mitochondrion.
The respiratory chain uses a variety of electron carriers:
Flavoproteins contain
Iron–sulfur proteins contain nonheme iron clusters such as FeS and

The respiratory chain uses a variety of electron carriers:
Coenzyme Q (ubiquinone, Q)
Cytochromes contain hemes

Iron–Sulfur Clusters
Figure 14.4 Structures of iron–sulfur clusters.
consist of nonheme iron complexed with thiol sulfurs of cysteine residues within a protein
Reduction potential of iron–sulfur clusters can vary greatly depending on the type of cluster and the protein environment

Coenzyme Q
Figure 14.5 Structure and oxidoreduction chemistry of coenzyme Q.
is a lipophilic electron carrier with a benzoquinone linked to an isoprene-containing tail
can transfer two electrons in one electron steps (via a stable semiquinone intermediate)
provides a link between two-electron carriers and one-electron carriers

Cytochromes (1 of 2)
Figure 14.6 Absorption spectra of cytochromes.
are classified based on their absorption spectra
The absorption spectra of the reduced forms of cytochromes b, c, and a are shown to right

Cytochrome c and
Hemes

Cytochrome a and

Standard Reduction Potentials of Major Electron Carriers in the Respiratory Chain (1 of 4)
Figure 14.8 Standard reduction potentials of the major respiratory electron carriers.
Transport of electrons through the respiratory chains can be visualized as a series of coupled exergonic reactions

Standard Reduction Potentials of Major Electron Carriers in the Respiratory Chain (2 of 4)
Three steps are sufficiently exergonic to drive ATP synthesis

Standard Reduction Potentials of Major Electron Carriers in the Respiratory Chain (3 of 4)
Free energy available from the oxidation of N A D H by
converted into a proton gradient that powers the synthesis of A T P from A D P and Pi

Standard Reduction Potentials of Major Electron Carriers in the Respiratory Chain (4 of 4)
Caveat! – these data are from standard conditions and may be very different from those in the mitochondrion

Multienzyme Complexes Involved in the Mitochondrial Respiratory Chain
Red arrows denote electron flow; gray arrows denote energy
released from complexes
used to drive
A T P synthesis
Figure 14.9 Multiprotein complexes in the mitochondrial respiratory assembly.

NADH–Coenzyme Q Reductase (Complex I) (1 of 2)
is a multisubunit complex of about 1000 kDa containing approximately 45 polypeptide chains
however, the bacterial complex contains only 14 “core” subunits
harbors one bound FMN (flavin mononucleotide) and eight iron–sulfur clusters
Electrons are transferred in multiple steps from N A D H to coenzyme Q (C o Q) via FMN and iron–sulfur clusters

NADH–Coenzyme Q Reductase (Complex I) (2 of 2)
Along with the electron transfer, protons are pumped across the inner membrane into the intermembrane space (I M S) of mitochondria:

Complex I Structure
Figure 14.9 Multiprotein complexes in the mitochondrial respiratory assembly.
The flow of two electrons from a single molecule of N A D H to C o Q causes conformational changes in complex I, which pumps four protons from the mitochondrial matrix to the I M S

Succinate–Coenzyme Q Reductase (Complex II)
In addition to accepting electrons from N A D H, C o Q can also accept electrons from intermediates in fatty acid oxidation and from succinate
Complex II (= succinate dehydrogenase) is an inner membrane multisubunit protein complex,which is also part of the citric acid cycle, transferring electrons from succinate through F A D and a series of iron–sulfur clusters to C o Q

Structure of Complex II
Figure 14.11 Structure of complex II (succinate–coenzyme Q reductase) from pig heart mitochondria (PDB ID: 1zoy).
Complex II transfers electrons from succinate through FAD and a series of iron–sulfur clusters to CoQ, but complex II does not pump protons into the IMS

Role of Coenzyme Q in Electron Transport
Figure 14.12 Coenzyme Q collects electrons from multiple flavoproteins.
Coenzyme Q collects electrons from complex I, complex II and other flavoproteins for transfer to complex III

Coenzyme Q: Cytochrome c Oxidoreductase (Complex III)
Complex III catalyzes the transfer of electrons from
(reduced coenzyme Q) to cytochrome c in the intermembrane space
Mammalian complex III is about 250 kDa and functions as a dimer with each monomer composed of 10 or 11 protein chains

Structure of Complex III
Figure 14.13 Structure of complex III (coenzyme Q:cytochrome c oxidoreductase).
Redox centers in the complex are shown in the bovine structure at left

Path of Electrons from CoQH2 to Cytochrome c – the Q Cycle (1 of 2)
Figure 14.14 The Q cycle.

Path of Electrons from CoQH2 to Cytochrome c – the Q Cycle (2 of 2)
During the Q cycle a two-electron donor,
is
transferring electrons to one-electron acceptors
Each complex III monomer has two Q binding sites
This requires that the electrons transferred from
take different paths, and in two stages
The net process pumps four protons into the IMS

Cytochrome c Oxidase (Complex IV)
exists as a homodimer with each monomer consisting of 13 subunits
catalyzes the transfer of electrons from reduced cytochrome c to oxygen, and pumps two protons into the IMS for every two electrons transferred
The net reaction (for the transfer of four electrons):

Structure of Complex IV
Figure 14.15 Structure of cytochrome c oxidase
(complex IV).
14.4 Oxidative Phosphorylation

Phosphate-to-Oxygen (P/O) Ratio (1 of 2)
The P/O ratio is the number of ATP molecules synthesized per pair of electrons carried though electron transport
Consider the transfer of electrons from NADH to

Phosphate-to-Oxygen (P/O) Ratio (2 of 2)
The P/O ratio for the transfer of electrons from NADH is about 2.5; from succinate (through complex II) about 1.5
P/O ratios may be non-integers because the oxidation of NADH and succinate is not directly coupled to ATP synthesis

Identification of “Coupling Sites” in Electron Transport
Figure 14.16 Experimental identification of “coupling sites.”
Experiments using inhibitors and artificial electron donors (e.g., ascorbate) demonstrated that complexes I, III, and IV were capable of driving ATP synthesis, but not complex II

Chemiosmotic Coupling and ATP Synthesis (1 of 2)
Figure 14.17 Chemiosmotic coupling of electron transport and ATP synthesis.
Chemiosmotic coupling model (Mitchell, 1961): electron transport drives the active transport of protons from the mitochondrial matrix to the intermembrane space; creating an electrochemical gradient.

Chemiosmotic Coupling and ATP Synthesis (2 of 2)
Figure 14.17 Chemiosmotic coupling of electron transport and ATP synthesis.
The dissipation of the electrochemical gradient through complex V provides the free energy for ATP synthesis

Evidence for Chemiosmotic Coupling
pH gradient and membrane potential both contribute to an electrochemical gradient (protonmotive force, pmf)
The free energy change of an electrochemical gradient is given by:
where
and
are the concentrations of ions in the two
compartments, Z is the charge on the ion, and ψ is the membrane potential (in volts)

Experimental Evidence for Chemiosmotic Coupling
Key electron transport proteins span the inner membrane
Intact membranes are necessary for oxidative phosphorylation
Uncoupling compounds, such as DNP:
allow electron transport to occur without generating ATP
act as ionophores and dissipate the proton gradient

Complex V is also called the
complex

c-ring (10 subunits in yeast, 8 in higher eukaryotes)

subunit also consists of b, d,
and OSCP
subunits which, along with a, comprise the stator (which prevents the rotation of the three αβ dimers of

complex.

knob is composed of three αβ dimers arranged
around a central stalk (the “rotor”) consisting of the γ, δ, and ε subunits

ATP synthase

Binding-Change Model for ATP Synthase (1 of 4)
Figure 14.21 Binding-change model for ATP synthase.
The three αβ dimers of
exist in three different
conformations, L (loose), T (tight), and O (open)

Binding-Change Model for ATP Synthase (2 of 4)
Figure 14.21 Binding-change model for ATP synthase.
The γ subunit rotates counterclockwise (driven by the passage of protons through
while the
components
are held in a fixed position by the stator

Binding-Change Model for ATP Synthase (3 of 4)
Figure 14.21 Binding-change model for ATP synthase.
As the γ subunit rotates, it interacts differently with each subunit and simultaneously causes conformational changes in all three dimers

Binding-Change Model for ATP Synthase (4 of 4)
Figure 14.21 Binding-change model for ATP synthase.
These conformational changes allow for the binding of ADP and Pi, the synthesis of ATP, and the release of ATP

Evidence for the Rotation of the γ Subunit (1 of 2)
Figure 14.22 An experimental system for observing rotation of the
component of
ATP synthase.
The cloned gene encoding
subunit was modified
with a His-tag to allow binding to a nickel-coated bead immobilized on a glass coverslip
A fluorescent-labeled actin filament was attached to the γ subunit

Figure 14.22 An experimental system for observing rotation of the
component of
ATP synthase.
The addition of ATP to this complex caused the hydrolysis of ATP, and the rotation of the γ subunit within the complex was visualized by fluorescence microscopy (below)

component of

14.5 Respiratory States and Respiratory Control

Respiratory Control by ADP and Pi (1 of 4)
Figure 14.24 Experimental demonstration of respiratory control.
Electrons are quantitated by oxygen uptake as measured
in μmol of O
ATP synthesis is quantitated in terms of phosphate incorporation into ATP

Respiratory Control by ADP and Pi (2 of 4)
Figure 14.24 Experimental demonstration of respiratory control.
Isolated mitochondria are placed in a chamber with 0.5
μmol of
Mitochondria take up oxygen during respiration

Respiratory Control by ADP and Pi (3 of 4)
Figure 14.24 Experimental demonstration of respiratory control.
The slow uptake at the beginning of the experiment reflects the oxidation of substrates in the mitochondria, and the addition of additional substrate (glutamate) does not
affect the rate of
consumption unless ADP + Pi are present

Respiratory Control by ADP and Pi (4 o 4)
Figure 14.24 Experimental demonstration of respiratory control.
The addition of ADP and Pi stimulates respiration
The second addition of ADP and Pi is twice the amount of the first, showing that the
magnitude of
uptake is
stoichiometric

Effects of an Inhibitor and Uncoupler on O2 Consumption and ATP Synthesis (1 of 3)
Figure 14.25 Effects of an inhibitor and an uncoupler on oxygen uptake and ATP synthesis.
In a similar experiment, the addition of ADP results in the production of ATP and an
increase in
consumption

Effects of an Inhibitor and Uncoupler on O2 Consumption and ATP Synthesis (2 of 3)
Figure 14.25 Effects of an inhibitor and an uncoupler on oxygen uptake and ATP synthesis.
Addition of the ATP synthase inhibitor oligomycin slows the
consumption rate and
prevents ATP synthesis (protons cannot flow through
causing proton pressure
to build up so that electron transport can no longer transfer protons to the IMS)

Effects of an Inhibitor and Uncoupler on O2 Consumption and ATP Synthesis (3 of 3)
Figure 14.25 Effects of an inhibitor and an uncoupler on oxygen uptake and A T P synthesis.
The addition of D N P, an
uncoupler, results in an
transport chain increases), but there remains no synthesis of A T P (because the uncoupler destroys the proton gradient)

Blocking Electron Transport by Inhibitors of ATP Synthase
At right is shown a hand-operated pump; the handle and water flow are mechanically coupled
If we were to hook a hose to the spigot and force water backward through the pump, the handle would move up and down; if we hold the handle, water flow would stop and water pressure would build
14.6 Mitochondrial Transport Systems

Transport Through Mitochondrial Membranes (1 of 3)
Figure 14.26 Major inner membrane transport systems for respiratory substrates and products.
The outer mitochondrial membrane is freely permeable to most small molecules up to about 5000 Da, but inner membrane permeability is severely limited

Transport Through Mitochondrial Membranes (2 of 3)
Figure 14.26 Major inner membrane transport systems for respiratory substrates and products.
Thus, transport across the inner membrane requires a transport system

Transport Through Mitochondrial Membranes (3 of 3)
Figure 14.26 Major inner membrane transport systems for respiratory substrates and products.
As the matrix is negatively charged relative to the cytoplasm, transporters utilize cotransport with protons or exchange with hydroxide ion to import metabolites

Transport of Reducing Equivalents
Figure 14.27 Shuttles for transfer of reducing equivalents from cytosol into mitochondria.
14.7 Energy Yields from Oxidative Metabolism

Energy Yields from Oxidative Phosphorylation (1 of 2)
Per mole of glucose, 4 moles of ATP are generated, plus
10 moles NADH and 2 moles
from glycolysis
through oxidative phosphorylation
Using the P/O ratios of 2.5 for NADH and 1.5 for
we calculate that the total ATP yield is 4 + (2.5 x 10)
+ (1.5 x 2) = 32 moles of ATP per mole of glucose oxidized
Energy Yields from Oxidative Phosphorylation (2 of 2)
14.8 The Mitochondrial Genome, Evolution, and Disease

Respiratory Chain Subunits Encoded by Mitochondrial or Nuclear DNA
Figure 14.28 Mitochondrial DNA and mitochondrial diseases.

Morbidity Map of Human Mitochondrial DNA
Figure 14.28 Mitochondrial DNA and mitochondrial diseases.
14.9 Oxygen as a Substrate for Other Metabolic Reactions

Oxidative Stress and Reactive Oxygen Species
Figure 14.29 Reactive oxygen species (ROS).
Oxidases often generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can damage biological molecules
Organisms may prevent biological damage through antioxidant compounds (nonenzymatic means) or enzymatic means such as superoxide dismutase (SOD)
The antioxidant vitamin E
The SOD reaction

Chapter 14 Summary (1 of 2)
Oxidative phosphorylation converts most of the energy captured from oxidative reactions to ATP
this process takes place in the mitochondria
ATP synthesis during oxidative phosphorylation is coupled
to the electron transfer from NADH and
This electron transfer is performed by the respiratory chain, comprising of four multisubunit protein complexes called complexes I–IV as well as ubiquinone/ubiquinol and cytochrome c

Chapter 14 Summary (2 of 2)
Complex Ⅰ, Ⅲ, and Ⅳpump protons from the mitochondrial matrix across the inner membrane, generating an electrochemical gradient termed proton motive force
This proton motive force drives the synthesis of ATP from ADP and Pi by ATP synthase (complex V)
about 1.5 molecules of ATP are produced per molecule
of
oxidized, while about 2.5 molecules of ATP
can be obtained per molecule NADH oxidized
